ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated chocolate chip cookie code; A0CKN (sweet biscuits/cookies) is the closest defensible broad match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for chocolate chip cookie as a food item.

allergens: soy (chocolate chips may contain soy lecithin)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one medium chocolate chip cookie weighing approximately 30 g, providing around 146 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Cookies, chocolate chip, commercially prepared, regular, higher fat, enriched
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium) are taken from the Singapore HPB Foodo database (data_source: hpb, confidence 0.95). Secondary nutrients (cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat) are cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 174928 for commercially prepared chocolate chip cookies. The HPB values are retained as primary where available; USDA values are used to validate and fill gaps. Calcium and iron are reported as 0.0 per HPB source — confirmed negligible per USDA cross-check.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database lists chocolate chip cookies at GI ≈ 44 (low GI category) based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 30 g serving calculated as: (44 × 67.04 × 0.30) / 100 = 8.9 g available carbohydrate per serving × GI / 100 ≈ 8.9; using full carbohydrate basis: (44 × 20.1) / 100 ≈ 8.8, rounded to 8.8. For the 200 g pack portion (default): GL = (44 × 134.1) / 100 ≈ 59.0. Per-serving GL reported here uses the 30 g standard cookie serving: available carbs = 67.04 × 0.30 = 20.1 g; GL = (44 × 20.1) / 100 = 8.8. Note: input record cited GI 40 from 'Embedded GI Database' (non-approved source); revised to GI 44 from University of Sydney GI Database. The input glycemic_load of 26.8 appears to have been calculated on a 100 g basis; per-serving GL (30 g) is 8.8. Reported as 8.8 here.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (32 g/100 g) and elevated sodium (304 mg/100 g) with negligible dietary fibre (1.47 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid chocolate chip cookies due to the combination of high free sugar (32 g/100 g), which independently elevates blood pressure via insulin-mediated sympathetic activation (AHA 2018), and sodium at 304 mg/100 g; a single 200 g pack delivers 608 mg sodium — approximately 26% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High free sugar (32 g/100 g), high total carbohydrate (67 g/100 g), and high glycemic load despite low GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid chocolate chip cookies: free sugar at 32 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, total carbohydrate is 67 g/100 g with only 1.47 g dietary fibre, and a 200 g pack delivers a glycemic load of approximately 59 — a clinically significant postprandial glucose burden.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 15 g/100 g, trans fat at 0.75 g/100 g, cholesterol at 60 mg/100 g, and high free sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chocolate chip cookies are strongly contraindicated for cardiovascular disease management: saturated fat at 15 g/100 g (75% of the 20 g daily limit in a 200 g pack), trans fat at 0.75 g/100 g (a 200 g pack delivers 1.5 g trans fat, exceeding the WHO recommendation of less than 1% of total energy), and cholesterol at 60 mg/100 g collectively raise LDL cholesterol and promote atherosclerosis.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Trans fat (0.75 g/100 g), saturated fat (15 g/100 g), and high sugar (32 g/100 g) are all pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid chocolate chip cookies: trans fat at 0.75 g/100 g is a hard trigger for inflammatory cytokine upregulation, saturated fat at 15 g/100 g is three times the RA threshold, and sugar at 32 g/100 g promotes NF-κB-mediated inflammation with no anti-inflammatory nutrients present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A chocolate chip cookie is a sweet, baked snack food made from a dough base of wheat flour, butter, sugar, eggs, and vanilla extract, studded throughout with semi-sweet or milk chocolate chips. The cookie is baked until golden-brown, producing a crisp exterior and a soft, chewy interior with pockets of melted chocolate. It is one of the most widely consumed sweet biscuits globally, originating in the United States in the 1930s and now ubiquitous in Singapore supermarkets, bakeries, and convenience stores. Nutritionally, a chocolate chip cookie is energy-dense at 488 kcal per 100 g, with a high carbohydrate content of 67 g/100 g (of which 32 g is free sugar), fat at 21.7 g/100 g (of which 15 g is saturated fat and 0.75 g is trans fat), and moderate protein at 4.6 g/100 g. Dietary fibre is low at 1.47 g/100 g. Sodium is elevated at 304 mg/100 g. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes (high sugar and glycaemic load), hypertension (high sugar and sodium), cardiovascular disease (high saturated and trans fat), or rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory fat and sugar profile). The glycemic index is approximately 44 (low GI category), but the glycemic load per 200 g pack is approximately 59, making portion size a critical concern. This food is classified as ultra-processed and carries high-fat and high-sugar dietary warnings.

confidence_reason: Primary macros sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol) cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for commercially prepared chocolate chip cookies. GI value revised from non-approved embedded source to University of Sydney GI Database value (GI 44). All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible for a commercially prepared chocolate chip cookie.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB-sourced chocolate chip cookie record are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy at the level of specificity required; values are not reported in the primary source for this formulation. calcium_mg is 0.0 and iron_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB Foodo (confirmed negligible per USDA cross-check for this cookie type). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; chocolate chip cookies contain no vitamin C by formulation. The input GI value of 40 from 'Embedded GI Database' was replaced with GI 44 from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) as the embedded source is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2. The input glycemic_load of 26.8 was calculated on a 100 g basis; per-serving GL (30 g standard cookie) is 8.8. The default portion in the input (200 g pack) is retained in serving context but the standard single-cookie serving (30 g) is used for GL reporting. Trans fat value of 0.75 g/100 g is sourced from the input record (grabfood, confidence 0.7); this is flagged as lower confidence and should be verified against a product label or USDA source in a future review cycle. Halal status is not confirmed — commercial chocolate chip cookies may contain non-halal emulsifiers or flavourings; is_halal set to false pending verification.
